title: Hyponatremia

sections.title: Definition & Epidemiology
sections.content: Hyponatremia is serum sodium <135 mEq/L, the most common electrolyte disorder in hospitalized patients. It reflects water excess relative to sodium, not necessarily sodium deficiency.
keyPoints: Associated with increased mortality, longer LOS, falls, fractures
Symptoms correlate with rate of development more than absolute level
Acute (<48h) vs Chronic (>48h) - critical distinction for treatment

sections.title: Diagnostic Algorithm
sections.content: A systematic approach based on volume status and urine studies identifies the cause.

subsections.name: Step 1: Confirm True Hyponatremia (Check Serum Osmolality)
items: Low Osm (<280): TRUE hypotonic hyponatremia - proceed to step 2
Normal Osm (280-295): Pseudohyponatremia (lab artifact from high lipids/proteins)
High Osm (>295): Translocational - glucose pulls water into vascular space
Corrected Na = Measured Na + 1.6 × [(Glucose - 100)/100]

subsections.name: Step 2: Assess Volume Status
items: HYPOVOLEMIC: orthostatic hypotension, tachycardia, dry mucosa, poor skin turgor
EUVOLEMIC: no signs of volume excess or depletion
HYPERVOLEMIC: edema, ascites, JVD, pulmonary congestion

subsections.name: Step 3: Check Urine Studies
items: Urine Osmolality: >100 = impaired water excretion | <100 = appropriate dilution (psychogenic polydipsia, beer potomania)
Urine Sodium: >30 = renal salt wasting | <30 = extrarenal loss or edematous states

sections.title: Causes by Volume Status
sections.content: Once volume status is determined, specific causes can be identified.

subsections.name: Hypovolemic Hyponatremia (Na + water loss, water retained)
items: UNa >30: Renal losses - diuretics (especially thiazides), salt-wasting nephropathy, Addison's disease, cerebral salt wasting
UNa <30: Extrarenal losses - GI (vomiting, diarrhea), third-spacing, burns, sweating

subsections.name: Euvolemic Hyponatremia
items: SIADH (most common): UOsm >100, UNa >30, clinically euvolemic, uric acid low
Hypothyroidism: check TSH
Adrenal insufficiency: check cortisol (Addison's can mimic SIADH)
Psychogenic polydipsia: UOsm <100 (maximally dilute), history of excessive water intake
Beer potomania/tea-and-toast: low solute intake, UOsm <100

subsections.name: Hypervolemic Hyponatremia (Total body Na and water increased, but water > Na)
items: Heart failure: poor renal perfusion, ADH release
Cirrhosis: splanchnic vasodilation, effective arterial underfilling
Nephrotic syndrome: third-spacing, low oncotic pressure
Advanced kidney disease: inability to excrete free water
UNa typically <30 (except in kidney disease)

sections.title: SIADH: The Most Common Cause
sections.content: SIADH (Syndrome of Inappropriate ADH) is the most common cause of euvolemic hyponatremia. Diagnosis requires excluding hypothyroidism and adrenal insufficiency.
keyPoints: Diagnostic criteria: Serum Osm <275, UOsm >100, UNa >30, euvolemic, normal thyroid/adrenal
Causes (remember HEAD): Head trauma, Ectopic production (lung cancer), Adverse drug effects, Diseases (pulmonary, CNS)
Common drugs: SSRIs, carbamazepine, vincristine, cyclophosphamide, NSAIDs, opioids
Cancers: Small cell lung cancer most common, also pancreatic, lymphoma
Pulmonary: pneumonia, TB, COPD, positive pressure ventilation

sections.title: Treatment: Rate Matters!
sections.content: Treatment depends on symptom severity and chronicity. Overcorrection causes osmotic demyelination syndrome (ODS).

subsections.name: Acute Severe Symptomatic (<48h, seizures, coma)
items: Medical emergency - risk of brain herniation > risk of ODS
3% hypertonic saline 100-150mL IV bolus over 10-20 minutes
Goal: raise Na 4-6 mEq/L in first 1-2 hours to stop symptoms
Can repeat bolus 1-2 times if still symptomatic
Then slow correction: no more than 10 mEq/L in first 24h total

subsections.name: Chronic Asymptomatic/Mild Symptoms
items: Brain has adapted - SLOW correction mandatory
Goal: 4-6 mEq/L per 24 hours, max 8 mEq/L in any 24h period
High risk for ODS: Na <105, hypokalemia, alcoholism, malnutrition, liver disease
If high risk for ODS: limit to 4-6 mEq/L per 24h

subsections.name: Treatment by Volume Status
items: Hypovolemic: isotonic saline (0.9% NS) - volume repletes AND corrects Na
Euvolemic (SIADH): fluid restriction (800-1000mL/day), salt tabs, +/- tolvaptan (vaptans), demeclocycline, or loop diuretic + salt
Hypervolemic: fluid and sodium restriction, treat underlying disease, diuretics
AVOID hypotonic fluids in all cases

sections.title: Osmotic Demyelination Syndrome (ODS)
sections.content: ODS (formerly central pontine myelinolysis) occurs with overly rapid sodium correction. It causes devastating, often permanent neurologic injury.
keyPoints: Risk: chronic hyponatremia corrected too fast (>10-12 mEq/L in 24h)
Pathophysiology: brain adapts to low Na by losing organic osmolytes; rapid correction causes water shift OUT of brain cells
Symptoms: 2-6 days after correction - quadriplegia, pseudobulbar palsy, locked-in syndrome
Location: central pons (classic), but can be extrapontine
Treatment: if overcorrection detected, LOWER sodium back with D5W +/- desmopressin
Prevention: slow correction, frequent Na monitoring (q2-4h initially)

keyTakeaways: Hyponatremia is about WATER excess, not sodium deficiency
Step 1: Check serum Osm to confirm true hyponatremia
Step 3: Urine Osm (<100 = appropriate dilution) and UNa
SIADH = euvolemic + UOsm >100 + UNa >30 + normal thyroid/adrenal
Acute symptomatic: 3% saline bolus for emergency
Chronic: SLOW correction (4-8 mEq/L per 24h) to avoid ODS
Overcorrection is dangerous - can re-lower Na with D5W + desmopressin

reviewQuestions.question: A 70-year-old on HCTZ presents with Na 118, serum Osm 255, orthostatic hypotension, UNa 45, UOsm 500. What is the cause?
reviewQuestions.explanation: Hypovolemic (orthostatic) + high UNa (>30) = renal salt wasting. Thiazides are the classic cause. UNa is high because the kidney is inappropriately wasting sodium. Treat with NS to volume replete (and stop the thiazide!).

reviewQuestions.question: A patient with SIADH and Na 126 is treated aggressively. Na rises from 118 to 132 in 18 hours. You're concerned about ODS. What do you do?
options: Give D5W +/- desmopressin to re-lower sodium
reviewQuestions.explanation: This is overcorrection (14 mEq/L in 18h when goal is 8-10/24h). To prevent ODS, you must RE-LOWER sodium using D5W infusion +/- desmopressin (which causes water retention). The goal is to slow down the rate of correction.

reviewQuestions.question: Which finding distinguishes SIADH from hypovolemic hyponatremia?
reviewQuestions.explanation: Both SIADH and some hypovolemic causes (like diuretics) have high UNa and concentrated urine. The KEY distinction is clinical volume status: SIADH is euvolemic, while diuretics cause hypovolemia. Always assess volume status!

references: Spasovski G, et al. Clinical Practice Guideline on Hyponatremia. European J Endocrinol 2014
Verbalis JG, et al. Diagnosis and Treatment of Hyponatremia: Expert Panel Recommendations. Am J Med 2013
Sterns RH. Disorders of Plasma Sodium. NEJM 2015
UpToDate: Overview of the treatment of hyponatremia in adults
